root_motivation: When a file on this laptop changes, the user (Joe) needs to know who changed it (claude-session vs user-interactive vs package-manager vs unknown), when, and with what confidence, so trust calibration and forensics stop requiring three Bash invocations and guesswork. The chain underneath this surface request: stat+mtime answer 'when' but not 'who/why'; ctrace knows PID+comm of writes; Claude session JSONLs know which turn/tool ran; fsstory is the read-only joiner over those sources that produces an attributed per-path timeline.
user_persona: Joe Yen, a single user on one Arch Linux laptop, running Claude Code sessions throughout the day. Consumes fsstory both directly via CLI and indirectly via /self-review Phase A. No multi-user, no remote hosts, no real-time stream — snapshot-on-demand queries only.

unfakeable_metric.name: acceptance_tests_passing
unfakeable_metric.harness_command: cargo test --workspace --tests --no-fail-fast -q 2>&1 | awk '/test result:/ {p+=$4} END {print p+0}'

acceptance_criteria.description: `fsstory path <path>` emits a JSON object with `path` and `events[]`, where each event has `ts`, `actor`, `op`, and `confidence` fields. Schema is stable enough that a test can parse it.

Given a synthetic ctrace ndjson log containing a write event on `<path>` from `comm=claude` and a matching Claude session JSONL with an Edit tool_use on the same `<path>` in the same time window, `fsstory path <path>` attributes that event to `claude-session:<jsonl-basename>:<turn-index>` with `confidence: high`.

When no ctrace log is available (graceful degradation), `fsstory path <path>` still emits an event list derived from stat mtime + path heuristics, with `confidence: low`, and exits 0. It never errors solely because ctrace was off.
`fsstory who-wrote <path>` exits 0 and emits exactly one line: the latest attributed event for `<path>` formatted as `<ts>\t<actor>\t<confidence>`.

Read-only invariant: across the full test suite, fsstory does not write to or modify any file under the source directories it ingests (ctrace logs, Claude project JSONLs, pacman log). Verified by a tempdir fixture + per-file mtime/size snapshot before-and-after.
A ctrace event whose `comm` matches a known interactive editor (vim/nvim/code/zed/helix) and has no claude ancestor PID resolves to `actor: user-interactive` with `confidence: medium`.
`fsstory summary --root <dir> --since 24h` emits a by-actor histogram of file changes under `<dir>`, mirroring the /self-review Phase A breakdown.
The CLI's `--format json` output for `fsstory path` and `fsstory ls` is stable enough across runs that two consecutive invocations on the same fixture produce identical byte-streams.

scope: Streaming ndjson parser over ctrace session logs at ~/.cache/ctrace/sessions/*.ndjson.
Streaming JSONL parser over Claude project transcripts at ~/.claude/projects/*/[uuid].jsonl, extracting Edit/Write/NotebookEdit/Bash tool_use turns with their paths.
Pacman log parser over /var/log/pacman.log (transaction-collapsed by default, --expand opt-in).
stat() fallback when no ndjson/JSONL covers an event.
An attributor module that joins (path, time-window, PID/comm, tool-name) into a single Attributed event with confidence ∈ {high, medium, low}.
Five CLI subcommands: path, ls, summary, who-wrote, diff.
JSON and human-readable text output formats.
non_goals: Modifying any data source. fsstory is strictly read-only over ctrace, JSONLs, pacman, journald, stat.
Real-time event streaming. Snapshot-on-demand only — no inotify, no fanotify, no long-running daemon.
Cross-host or multi-user attribution. Single laptop, single user.
Persistent index in v1. SQLite cache is Phase 3, out of scope here.
Inferring intent from diff content. We surface the session turn's English description; we do not LLM-summarize the diff.
Replacing git blame. For tracked repo files we can call `git log -- <path>` but we do not reimplement git's algorithms.
Tamper detection. If a JSONL or ctrace log is hand-edited we report what's in the file.
Web UI, TUI, or daemon. CLI-only.

additional.phase_scope: Phase 0 of the PRD: ctrace + jsonl sources, attributor with claude-session/claude-bash/unknown, path + who-wrote commands. Phases 1-3 are explicit non-goals for this run.

five_whys_trace.q: Why does Joe want a file-attribution timeline tool?
five_whys_trace.a: Because stat + git log don't answer 'who last touched this and through which Claude session / which tool', so trust calibration and forensics today require three commands and guesswork.

five_whys_trace.q: Why is guessing about file authorship a real problem and not a nice-to-have?
five_whys_trace.a: Concrete moments: an autobuilder reviewer flagged unauthorized drift on agent/intent-card.json and reconstructing the chain took three Bash calls; /self-review Phase A says 'N files changed' with no actor breakdown; a config file looks unfamiliar and the only honest answer is 'I don't know'.

five_whys_trace.q: Why can't existing tools (stat, git log, ctrace alone, JSONL grep alone) already answer this?
five_whys_trace.a: Each holds part of the answer. ctrace has PID+comm but not the Claude session turn that caused the bash invocation. JSONLs have turn-level intent but not the PIDs they spawned. Pacman has package events but no per-file timeline aligned with the others. The missing layer is a joiner across all four, with a confidence score so low-evidence answers don't get treated like high-evidence answers.

five_whys_trace.q: Why a per-path timeline rather than a global event log?
five_whys_trace.a: The questions Joe actually asks are path-scoped: 'who last touched <this file>?', 'what did I change under <this dir>?'. A global log shifts the join work onto the user every time. The data sources are already global event logs — fsstory's value is path-scoped querying with attribution.

five_whys_trace.q: Why ship v0 as a snapshot CLI rather than a daemon with a persistent index?
five_whys_trace.a: Snapshot-on-demand is correct by construction (no stale-index risk) and matches Joe's usage cadence (a handful of queries per day, mostly at /self-review time). The SQLite index in PRD Phase 3 is a perf optimization for week+ queries, not a correctness requirement.

ambiguities_resolved.question: The PRD lists five subcommands across phases 0-1. Which are MUST for this run?
ambiguities_resolved.resolution: Per PRD §7 Phasing, Phase 0 = `path` + `who-wrote` + attribution for claude-session / claude-bash / unknown. Those are the MUST ACs. `summary` is SHOULD (Phase 1 territory but useful early). `ls` and `diff` are deferred.

ambiguities_resolved.question: Does v1 implement the SQLite cache from Phase 3?
ambiguities_resolved.resolution: No. Listed as a non-goal. v1 is per-query in-memory join only.

ambiguities_resolved.question: How does fsstory behave when ctrace is off?
ambiguities_resolved.resolution: Graceful degradation per PRD §4.5: stat+path-heuristics only, confidence=low, exit 0, never error on missing ctrace. Encoded as AC3.

ambiguities_resolved.question: Should /self-review integration ship in this run?
ambiguities_resolved.resolution: The fsstory side (a `summary` subcommand emitting the by-actor breakdown) is SHOULD-level AC7. Editing /self-review itself is out of scope for this run; that's a follow-up wiring task.
